title: Visual Search System - System Design Interview
tagline: Design image-to-image search using embeddings, ANN retrieval, and ranking feedback.
problem: Build a visual search system where a user submits an image or crop and receives visually similar images from a corpus of 100-200 billion images, ranked quickly without personalization.

functional: Accept an image or crop as the query.
Return visually similar images ranked by similarity.
Use image pixels rather than metadata for the core retrieval task.
Refresh indexes as new images arrive.
nonFunctional: Low-latency retrieval at very large corpus size.
High recall from approximate nearest neighbor search.
requirements.designPrompt: Teach this as an ML retrieval system: query preprocessing, embedding model, index building, ANN retrieval, optional reranking, and evaluation.

capacity.label: Corpus size
capacity.value: 100B+ images requires ANN; exact scan is infeasible.

capacity.label: Embedding size
capacity.value: Hundreds to low thousands of dimensions, compressed for index memory.

capacity.label: Serving stages
capacity.value: Encode query, ANN retrieve candidates, rerank/filter, return results.

capacity.label: Feedback
capacity.value: Clicks are noisy positives and need debiasing.

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: 100 to 200 billion indexed images. Queries can be whole images or user-selected crops. Embedding vectors are precomputed for indexed images. Online query embedding must be fast enough for interactive search.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: Compute why exact nearest neighbor is impossible at 100B scale, then justify approximate indexing and a two-stage ranking path.

dataModel.name: image_asset
dataModel.note: Indexed image metadata and storage location.

dataModel.name: image_embedding
dataModel.note: Vector representation for an image or crop.

dataModel.name: training_pair
dataModel.note: Positive or negative image pair used for model training.

steps.title: 1. Frame Visual Search as Embedding Retrieval
steps.description: The model maps visually similar images near each other in vector space. Search becomes nearest-neighbor retrieval against precomputed image embeddings.
steps.decisionPrompt: What does relevance mean when the query is an image rather than text?

concepts.definition: A dense vector representation learned by a model.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns images into comparable numeric points.

concepts.definition: A distance or similarity measure such as cosine similarity.
concepts.whyItMatters: It ranks visually close images.

concepts.definition: Training a model to produce useful embeddings.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the core ML task in visual search.

deepDives: Pixels are the core signal; metadata can be used later for filtering but should not define the main search.
bottlenecks: Image encoding latency in the request path.
talkingPoints: State that exact nearest neighbor over 100B vectors is too slow.

steps.title: 2. Build Training Data From Images and Clicks
steps.description: The corpus provides images; clicks and human judgments provide weak labels for which results were useful. Self-supervised and contrastive methods can supplement sparse click labels.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where do positive and negative training pairs come from?

concepts.definition: A noisy label inferred from behavior such as a click.
concepts.whyItMatters: It scales better than manual labels but has bias.

concepts.definition: Two images that should be close in embedding space.
concepts.whyItMatters: It teaches similarity.

concepts.definition: A visually close but semantically wrong candidate.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves model discrimination.

deepDives: Clicks are position-biased, so evaluation should not blindly treat every click as perfect relevance.
bottlenecks: Sparse labels for rare visual categories.
Click feedback can reinforce current retrieval mistakes.
talkingPoints: Mention self-supervised pretraining as a way to use unlabeled images.

steps.title: 3. Preprocess Images and Train an Encoder
steps.description: Images are resized, normalized, and passed through a CNN or vision transformer that outputs a fixed-size embedding for whole images and crops.
steps.decisionPrompt: What transformations must be consistent between training, indexing, and query serving?

concepts.definition: Resize, color normalization, and pixel scaling before model inference.
concepts.whyItMatters: Inconsistency here causes training-serving skew.

concepts.definition: A convolutional neural network for image representation.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is a proven baseline for visual features.

concepts.definition: A transformer architecture adapted to image patches.
concepts.whyItMatters: It can produce strong embeddings at higher compute cost.

deepDives: Crop queries should run through the same preprocessing and encoder path as indexed images.
bottlenecks: Different color pipelines can degrade quality.
talkingPoints: Name ResNet/CNN and ViT as reasonable model families.

steps.title: 4. Train With Contrastive Objectives
steps.description: Contrastive training pulls positive pairs together and pushes negatives apart. It is a natural fit when the serving task is nearest-neighbor retrieval.
steps.decisionPrompt: How does the model learn that two images are visually similar?

concepts.definition: A loss that rewards close positives and distant negatives.
concepts.whyItMatters: It directly shapes embedding space.

concepts.definition: A loss using anchor, positive, and negative examples.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is an intuitive contrastive formulation.

concepts.definition: Scaling vectors to comparable length.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes cosine or dot-product similarity more stable.

deepDives: Hard negatives make the model better, but mislabeled negatives can hurt training.
bottlenecks: Poor pair sampling limits model quality.
talkingPoints: Connect the loss to nearest-neighbor retrieval.

steps.title: 5. Build and Publish Embedding Indexes
steps.description: Offline or streaming workers compute embeddings for indexed images and build versioned ANN indexes. Versioning makes rollouts and rollback safe.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do new images become searchable without corrupting the active index?

concepts.definition: A job that transforms embeddings into a searchable ANN structure.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prepares data for low-latency retrieval.

concepts.definition: A named immutable index snapshot.
concepts.whyItMatters: It supports safe activation and rollback.

concepts.definition: Adding new embeddings between full rebuilds.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves freshness without rebuilding everything immediately.

deepDives: At 100B scale, index building is a distributed data pipeline, not a single in-memory job.
bottlenecks: Index build time and validation cost.

steps.title: 6. Retrieve Candidates With ANN Search
steps.description: Approximate nearest neighbor search trades a small amount of exactness for orders-of-magnitude faster retrieval. The service returns candidates for reranking.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why is approximate retrieval required, and which tradeoff does it introduce?

concepts.definition: Approximate nearest-neighbor search over vectors.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes 100B-scale retrieval practical.

concepts.definition: Higher recall usually costs more search time or memory.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the central ANN tuning decision.

concepts.definition: A partition of the vector index.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets retrieval scale horizontally.

options.title: Approximate nearest neighbor
options.description: Use ANN for interactive search at corpus scale.
tradeoffs: May miss some exact nearest neighbors.

options.title: Exact vector scan
options.description: Compare the query to every image vector.
tradeoffs: Infeasible latency and compute at 100B scale.

flows.name: Visual search request
flows.note: The online path encodes the query, searches ANN shards, and returns candidates.

deepDives: HNSW, IVF, or product quantization are implementation choices; the interview should focus on the recall/latency/memory tradeoff.
bottlenecks: Scatter-gather across many shards can hurt p99.
talkingPoints: Explain that reranking can improve precision after broad ANN retrieval.

steps.title: 7. Rerank, Filter, and Hydrate Results
steps.description: A reranker can use more expensive similarity, deduplication, quality filters, and policy filters over a smaller candidate set before returning display-ready results.
steps.decisionPrompt: What happens after ANN returns candidate IDs?

concepts.definition: A second-stage ranker over a smaller candidate set.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves precision without scoring the entire corpus.

concepts.definition: Removing near-identical or duplicate images.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes results more useful.

concepts.definition: Fetching display metadata and URLs for result IDs.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns model output into client-ready responses.

deepDives: Even when personalization is out of scope, filtering for deleted or unsafe images is still a serving concern.
bottlenecks: Hydrating thousands of candidates is too slow.
talkingPoints: Keep ANN recall high, then use reranking for precision.

steps.title: 8. Evaluate Quality and Refresh Models and Indexes
steps.description: Offline metrics such as precision@K, recall@K, MRR, and NDCG evaluate ranking quality. Online click metrics validate improvements before full rollout.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we know whether a new embedding model or ANN index is better?

concepts.definition: The fraction of top K results that are relevant.
concepts.whyItMatters: It measures visible result quality.

concepts.definition: A metric that rewards relevant results near the top with graded relevance.
concepts.whyItMatters: It fits ranked visual search results.

concepts.definition: An index tested without serving all production traffic.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces rollout risk.

deepDives: Index changes and model changes should be versioned together because embeddings are model-specific.
bottlenecks: Offline labels may not cover all visual categories.
Full re-embedding can be expensive after model upgrades.
talkingPoints: Close with a versioned pipeline from image ingestion to search serving.

functional.how: The query path preprocesses the image or crop with the same encoder family used for corpus embeddings, then searches ANN shards.
Corpus images are embedded offline into versioned sharded ANN indexes, avoiding exact scans over the full image set.
Contrastive training shapes embedding space, ANN retrieves broad candidates, and reranking/dedup filters improve top-result precision.
Clicks and judgments become weak labels, while offline metrics and online experiments govern model and index rollout.

nonFunctional.how: Query embedding, ANN scatter-gather, bounded reranking, and result hydration keep search responsive at corpus scale.
Embedding model versions, index versions, shadow evaluation, and activation controls prevent corrupt index rollouts.
Contrastive pairs, hard negatives, click debiasing, and NDCG-style evaluation improve visual relevance.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design is a two-sided ML retrieval system. Offline pipelines turn corpus images into model-versioned embeddings and sharded ANN indexes. Online requests preprocess the query image or crop, produce a query embedding, retrieve approximate nearest neighbors, rerank and filter candidates, hydrate result metadata, and log clicks for evaluation and future training.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk through both sides of the system: how an image enters the index, and how a query image turns into ranked results.

concepts.definition: Embedding and ANN index construction happen before queries arrive.
concepts.whyItMatters: At 100B+ images, online exact search is impossible.

concepts.definition: A query embedding probes ANN shards and reranks a bounded candidate set.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps search interactive while preserving result quality.

concepts.definition: Index vectors are produced by a specific model version.
concepts.whyItMatters: A model upgrade usually requires a coordinated re-embedding and index rollout.

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.

options.title: Simplified single-index view
options.description: Useful for explaining the idea quickly, but production needs sharding, versioning, evaluation, and click logging as shown in the full design.

flows.name: Image indexing
flows.note: New images are embedded offline and published into a versioned ANN index.

flows.name: Visual search query
flows.note: The online path encodes the query crop, retrieves ANN candidates, reranks, hydrates, and logs feedback.

deepDives: The core answer is not just a model. It is model training, corpus embedding, ANN index lifecycle, online retrieval, reranking, and evaluation.
bottlenecks: Full corpus re-embedding after model upgrades.
Noisy click labels and position bias.
talkingPoints: State why exact nearest neighbor cannot work at 100B scale.
Use versioned model and index rollouts.

followUps: How would you support personalization later?
How do you evaluate crop queries specifically?
How would you compress embeddings to reduce memory?
How do you handle model upgrades that require re-indexing everything?
